title: Appendix A — Git BASH
text:
A.1 Shortcut keys
Git for Windows is a set of tools for Windows users. Of main interest, Git for Windows provides a Bash emulation used to run Git from the command line. Using Git Bash, Windows users can use “git” commands exactly like one would do in Linux and Unix environments. Besides the full set of Git core commands, Git Bash is also packaged with additional shell commands that can be useful.
When you open Git Bash, you will see something like this:
The user_id@computer_id is an ID based on name of the user that is logged in, and the name of the device running Git Bash. The MINGW64 part is the name of the compiler used to build Git Bash. The ~ at the end of the first line indicates the current directory. A tilde means that the current directory is the home directory of the user.
An extensive list of available commands in Bash can be found here. Not all of them are available in Git Bash. Below is a short list of useful shortcut keys.
Windows users are used to using Ctrl+cCtrl+c and Ctrl+vCtrl+v to “Copy” and “Paste”, but these shortcuts are not available in Git Bash, since they are reserved for other commands. it is possible to enable “Copy” using Ctrl+Shift+cCtrl+Shift+c and “Paste” using Ctrl+Shift+vCtrl+Shift+v, by right-clicking in the terminal, navigating to Options>Keys, and checking the “Ctrl+Shift+letter shortcuts” box.

title: Appendix A — Git BASH
text:
Description
Shortcut




Moving the cursor



Go to beginning of line (Home)
Ctrl+a


Go to end of line (End)
Ctrl+e


Previous command (Up arrow)
Ctrl+p


Next command (Down arrow)
Ctrl+n


Go left/back one word
Alt+b


Go right/forward one word
Alt+f


Go forward one character
Ctrl+f


Go backward one character
Ctrl+b


Editing



Copy
Ctrl+ins


Paste
Shift+ins


Clear the screen
Ctrl+l


Delete the character before the cursor (Backspace)
Ctrl+h


Delete the character after the cursor
Ctrl+d


Process control



Interrupt/kill current process (SIGINT)
Ctrl+c

title: Appendix A — Git BASH
text:
A.2 Git BASH commands
It is not easy to understand what commands are (or are not) available in Git Bash, why they are (or are not) available, nor is it straight forward to find documentation on them. Some commands have documentation that can be accessed by typing help [command-name] in the terminal. More generally speaking, CMD, PowerShell, and BASH command documentation can be found many different places, for example here.
Here we will introduce a few basic commands most of which are used extensively in this tutorial. We will also provide some focused documentation that covers how the commands are used in this tutorial, alongside links to more extensive documentation.





Command
Description




cd
Change working directory


clear
Clear terminal window


echo > / echo >>
Create file / append content to file


ls
List directory content


mkdir
Create folder


pwd
Print working directory


rm
Remove files


start
Start a program/open a file/open a directory

title: Appendix A — Git BASH
text:
A.3 Note on command options
Most of the commands listed here can take different options, e.g. ls has an -a and a -1 option. It is possible to use multiple options at once on the form ls -a1 instead of the more verbose form ls -a -1.

A.3.1 cd
Syntax: cd [DIR]
Description: Change the current directory to DIR.
If DIR is not provided, changes the current directory to the value of the $HOME shell variable, i.e. the home directory of the user. cd - changes the working directory to the previous working directory ( “-” is converted to $OLDPWD).
Note that forward slashes (/) are used to specify file paths, and that paths can be both on the form C:/path/to/directory and /c/path/to/directory
If the file path contains spaces, enclose the file path in (double) quotes.
Git for Windows also comes with shell integration, so an alternative to using cd we can change the working directory by navigating to the folder in Windows explorer, right click on the folder (shift+right click on Windows 11) and select “Open Git Bash here”.
Link to documentation: https://ss64.com/bash/cd.html

A.3.1.1 Example - Change directory
We can use cd to change the working directory by providing a file path to the directory.
~
$ cd C:/path/to/directory
/c/path/to/directory
$

Note that the working directory was set as C:/path/to/directory, but is displayed on the form /c/path/to/directory


A.3.1.2 Example - file path with spaces
If a file path contains spaces, enclose the file path in (double) quotes.
~
$ cd "/c/path/to/some directory"
/c/path/to/some directory
$



A.3.1.3 Example - Change to subdirectory
To change the current working directory to a subdirectory of the current working directory, we can simple set DIR to the subdirectory name.
/c/path/to/dir
$ cd subfolder
/c/path/to/dir/subfolder
$



A.3.1.4 Example - Change to parent directory
We can change the working directory to the parent directory by setting DIR to “..”.
/c/path/to/dir
$ cd ..
/c/path/to
$

You can also use “..” more generally when specifying a file path, to denote the parent directory
/c/path/to/dir_a
$ cd ../dir_b
/c/path/to/dir_b
$



A.3.1.5 Example - Change to previous directory
We can change the working directory to the previous working directory by setting DIR to “-”.
/c/current/cd/dir
$ cd -
/c/previous/cd/dir
$



A.3.1.6 Example - Changing working directory to user’s home directory
We can set the current working directory to the user’s home directory by not specifying DIR, or by setting DIR to “~”.
/c/current/dir
$ cd ~
~
$ cd -
/c/current/dir
$ cd
~
$




A.3.2 clear
Syntax: clear
Description: Clear the terminal screen.
Link to documentation: No documentation available.

A.3.2.1 Example
Imagine we had the history shown in the terminal below, ending with a clear command.
~
$ cd C:/path/to/directory
/c/path/to/directory
$ clear

This would result in the terminal shown below.
/c/path/to/directory
$

Clearing the terminal screen can also be done using the shortcut key Ctrl+lCtrl+l



A.3.3 echo > / echo >>
Syntax: echo [STRING] >> [FILE]
Description: Append STRING content to FILE using the “>>” redirection operator. If the file does not exist it will be created. If “>>” is replaced with “>” then the given STRING overwrites the content of FILE.
Link to documentation: https://ss64.com/bash/echo.html

A.3.3.1 Example - Create empty text file
You can create an empty file by not providing any STRING and using the “>” redirection operator (this will overwrite the any existing file with the same name).
~
$ echo > empty_file.txt


empty_file.txt

⠀




A.3.3.2 Example - Create text file with content
We can create a new text file with some content in it (or overwrite an existing file) by using the “>” redirection operator and by providing a STRING with the content.
~
$ echo "Some content" > new_file.txt


new_file.txt

Some content




A.3.3.3 Example - Append content to existing text file
To append content to an existing text file use the “>>” redirection operator.
~
$ echo "Some content" > append_file.txt
~
$ echo "Some more content" >> append_file.txt


append_file.txt

Some content
Some more content





A.3.4 ls
Syntax: ls [OPTIONS] [DIR]
List directory content.
List directories and files in DIR. If DIR is not provided, lists the content of the current working directory. Content is listed alphabetically.
OPTIONS: -a Do not ignore entries starting with dot (.), i.e. hidden files. -1 List one file per line.
Link to documentation: https://ss64.com/bash/ls.html

A.3.4.1 Example - List content of work directory
If we want to see a list of content in the current work directory, we can use ls.
/c/some/dir
$ ls
dir_a/ file_a.txt file_c.txt file_e.txt
dir_b/ file_b.txt file_d.txt file_f.txt

By default multiple entries are listed in each line and directories are colour-coded and has a “/” suffix.


A.3.4.2 Example - List all content of a directory
By default, entries starting with a dot (.), i.e. hidden files, are ignored. Use the “-a” option to list all files.
/c/some/git_repo
$ ls -a
.git/ file_a.txt file_c.txt file_e.txt
.gitignore file_b.txt file_d.txt file_f.txt



A.3.4.3 Example - List one entry per line
We can use the “-1” option to list only one entry per line.
/c/some/dir
$ ls -1
dir_a/
dir_b/
file_a.txt
file_b.txt
file_c.txt
file_d.txt
file_e.txt
file_f.txt




A.3.5 mkdir
Syntax: mk [FOLDER]
Create folder.
Create a new folder with name FOLDER in the current working directory, if it does not already exists. If the folder name contains spaces enclose the name in quotes.
Link to documentation: https://ss64.com/bash/mkdir.html

A.3.5.1 Example
Create new folders. Note that we don’t have to enclose the folder name in quotes if it contains no spaces.
/c/some/dir
$ ls -1
file_a.txt
file_b.txt
/c/some/dir
$ mkdir new-folder
/c/some/dir
$ mkdir "new-folder"
/c/some/dir
$ ls -1
new-folder/
'new folder'/
file_a.txt
file_b.txt




A.3.6 pwd
Syntax: pwd
Print working directory.
Link to documentation: https://ss64.com/bash/pwd.html

A.3.6.1 Example
When working with Git this command is mainly used to see where the current user’s home directory is, since the current working directory is always shown in the terminal.
~
$ pwd
/c/absolute/path/to/home/directory




A.3.7 rm
Syntax: `rm [OPTIONS] [FILE]``
Remove files.
To remove a file you must have write permission on the file and the folder where it is stored.
By default, rm does not remove directories. Use the “-r” option to remove each listed directory too, along with all of its content.
You should, obviously, be VERY careful when deleting files using terminal commands. Especially if you delete directories and their contents recursively.
OPTIONS: -f Ignore non-existent files, never prompt. -r Remove directories and their contents recursively.
Link to documentation: https://ss64.com/bash/rm.html

A.3.7.1 Example - Remove file
/c/some/dir
$ ls
file_a.txt file_b.txt
/c/some/dir
$ rm file_a.txt
/c/some/dir
$ ls
file_b.txt




A.3.8 start
Syntax: start [FILE/DIR/PROGRAM]
Start a program, or open a file/directory in Windows Explorer.
TODO: Try to find some documentation on this command. This does not seem to be the CMD START command? Why does something likestart firefox actually work??? 
Link to documentation:

A.3.8.1 Example - Open current working directory in Windows explorer
We can open the current working directory in Windows explorer by simply using start .
/c/some/dir
$ start .





Chacon, Scott, and Ben Straub. 2021. “Pro Git.” https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2.

title: Appendix B — Graphical User Interfaces (GUI’s)
text:
Pro’s
There exists a plethora of GUI’s that can make using Git a more smooth experience. In many GUI’s you can point-and-click your way through everything you need to do, they can help trivialize complicated tasks, they can make beautiful graphical visualizations of complicated commit histories, and so on.
The general advice of this tutorial is to not use a GUI if you don’t need to, especially if you are new to Git. Learning Git using a GUI is shortsighted. It might make it easier to get started, but it can have a negative impact on your ability to understand how Git works, which might be problematic in the long run. In continuation of this, some programs that have nothing to do with Git also comes with functionalities to use Git from within the program. It is unclear why it would EVER be desirable to use Git like that. If you want to use a GUI, use a third-party program whose sole purpose is to be a Git GUI.
Below will will briefly list some of pro’s and con’s of using GUI’s and leave it at that.
Point-and-click interface In GUI’s you can point-and-click your way through everything, which makes using Git a more smooth and less technical experience, which is preferable to some people.
Facilitation of complicated tasks Some GUI’s can help with complicated tasks. For example, in some third-party GUI’s where you login using an account, you can setup your GUI account using your GitHub account, and the GUI will automatically authenticate you to your GitHub repositories. This process is a lot more technical and complicated to do yourself at the terminal.
Graphical visualizations of commit histories Not being able to make a (good) graphical visualization of your commit history might be the biggest limitation of using Git at the terminal. Any decent GUI will provide you with a beautiful graphical visualization of your commit history, which is especially valuable if you are working on a project with an extensive and complicated commit history.

title: Appendix B — Graphical User Interfaces (GUI’s)
text:
Con’s
Point-and-click interface Being able to point-and-click your way through everything means that you will most likely be doing exactly that. Which means that you will not learn any Git, and it will make it harder to remember things that you do actually know how to do at the terminal.
Facilitation complicated tasks Having the GUI do complicated tasks is nice, but again comes with the caveat that you yourself will not in fact learn anything.
Access to your preferred GUI If you are dependent on using a specific GUI to use Git, this might become problematic if you are all of a sudden forced to work in an environment where you do not have access to that GUI (or any other GUI for that matter). The way to make sure this is (probably) not a problem, is to actually learn how to use Git through terminal commands, so that you can always fall back to doing that if necessary.
Might only have access to a limited set of Git commands GUI’s do not come with a complete implementation of all Git commands. Some (advanced) Git commands are only available through terminal commands.




Chacon, Scott, and Ben Straub. 2021. “Pro Git.” https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2.

title: 5  GitHub
text:
5.1 What is GitHub?
In this chapter we will give a short introduction to GitHub.
Github is a host for Git repositories. There are many other sites that also hosts Git repositories, but Git is by far the most popular one. Hosting Git repositories online makes it easy to share code and collaborate with others.

title: 2  Introduction to Git
text:
2.1 What is Git?
In this chapter we will introduce Git. Before actually introducing any basic Git commands, we will use some time to try to explain some technical aspects of how Git works and how how it separates itself from other (distributed) VCS’s.
So what is Git and how did it come to be? Git is a distributed VCS, and it is born from controversy. In 2005 the relationship soured between the Linux kernel project and the company owning the proprietary distributed VCS, BitKeeper, that the Linux community was using as a VCS. This resulted in the free licence of BitKeeper that the Linux community was using being revoked. Prompted by this, the Linux developer community (in particular Linus Thorvald, the creator of Linux) developed their own tool to replace BitKeeper.
TODO: maybe add some details on what the goal of Git was, ie to better accomodate the needs of the Linux kernel project community.

title: 1  Introduction to Git
text:
1.2 What is Git?
Git is a distributed version control system (DVCS)
Software that tracks versions of files
Each version is a snapshot of the files at that point

Git is a distributed version control system (DVCS). A version control system (VCS) is software that tracks versions of files. In contrast to centralized VCS’s, A DVCS does not have a central code repository where files are stoed. Instead every user has the entire history of versions of files.
Git stores versions of files as “snapshots” in contrasts to many other VCS’s where differences between versions of files are stored.

title: 1  Introduction to Git
text:
1.3 Why should you use Git?
Manually storing old versions of code manually is haphazard and of limited use. Using Git can help with:

Switching back and forth between different versions of files
Seeing exact differences between versions of code
Facilitate working multiple people on the same code.

title: 1  Introduction to Git
text: 1.4 Installation

title: 2  Introduction to Git
text:
2.5 Where to get help
TODO: this section should probably come at a point where the first “git” command has been introduced.
If you need help with how to use a Git, all of the commands have a comprehensive manual page that can be accessed in multiple ways. One such way is to use the git help command:
$ git help <verb>

For example, if you want to access the manual page for the add command you can run the following command.
$ git help add

A concise version of the manual page for a command showing the available options can also be output by using the -h option with a command, like so
$ git add -h





Chacon, Scott, and Ben Straub. 2021. “Pro Git.” https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2.

Manually storing old versions of code manually is haphazard and of limited use. Using Git can help with:

title: 1  Introduction to Git
text:
1.4 Installating on Windows
To use Git on a Windows OS, we recommend installing Git for Windows, which can be downloaded here. Git for Windows provides a BASH emulation used to run Git form the command line which behaves just like the “git” command in LINUX and UNIX environments.

title: 1  Introduction to version control
text:
1.1.1 Local VCS’s
The first development toward better version control solutions was so-called local VCS’s. A local VCS is a local database on a single computer that keeps track of changes to files that are under revision control. Local VCS’s were a clear improvement over manual version control, and can be useful for small and/or personal projects. But these types of VCS’s still have important limitations.

All file version are stored locally, making it vulnerable to data loss.
It is difficult to collaborate with others using a local VCS, since changes can’t easily be shared or merged.




1.1.2 Centralized VCS’s
To overcome the limitations with collaborations using local VCS’s, the next type of VCS’s that were developed were so-called Centralized VCS’s. These systems work by have a single central server that contains all the code that is version controlled, and then developers can checkout code from that server and commit changes back to the server. This setup has many advantages over local VCS’s.

Developers knows to some degree of certainty what other people are doing.
Administrators can control what files each developer can access and modify.

However, centralized CVS’s also have downsides:

Single point of failure. If the server is temporarily offline, nobody can collaborate at all or save new versions of the code.
If the server dies and no proper backup has been kept, the entire history of the project is lost except whatever snapshorts people happen to have on their local machines.




1.1.3 Distributed VCS
The final type of VCS is distributed VCS’s. In a distributed VCS, developers don’t check out just check out the latest snapshot of the files, they make a copy of the entire history of the files. This is a way more flexible type of system that have several advantages over centralized VCS’s.

No single point of failure. Each developer has the entire history of version of files.
Instead of relying on a central server, developers can share and synchonize changes directly with each others.
Developers can work offline.
Allows for more flexible workflows that are not possible with a single centralized server.
Operations like branching and merging are faster since you don’t need to communicate with a server.

title: 1  Introduction to Git
text:
1.4 Installing Git on Windows
To use Git on Windows we recommend installing Git for Windows, which can be downloaded here. Git for Windows provides a BASH emulation used to run Git form the command line which behaves just like the “git” command in LINUX and UNIX environments.

title: 2  Introduction to Git
text:
2.2 Git background story
Git is born from controversy. In 2005 the relationship soured between the Linux kernel project and the company owning the proprietary distributed VCS, BitKeeper, that the Linux community was using. This resulted in the free licence of BitKeeper that the Linux community was using being revoked. Prompted by this, the Linux developer community (in particular Linus Thorvald, the creator of Linux) developed their own tool to replace BitKeeper.Linus Thorvald developed the first version of Git within 2 weeks, and was quickly adopted by the open-source community.

title: 1  Introduction to version control
text:
1.1 What is version control?
We will start this tutorial with a short introduction to version control in general, and introduce different types of version control systems that one can use.
Version control is the practice of organizing and tracking different versions of computer files over time. Generally, you can do this for any type of file, but it is primarily done for source code, i.e. plain text files. Using version control enables viewing old versions of files, reverting a file to a previous version etc.
A version control system (VCS) is a software tool that automates version control. You might already use a “VCS” without realizing it. The common practice of copying files into another directory is the most simple form of version control. It is a very simple and straight-forward approach to version control, but it is also very error prone and of limited use.

title: 1  Introduction to version control
text:
1.1.1 Local VCS
The first development toward better version control solutions was so-called local VCS’s. A local VCS is a local database on a single computer that keeps track of changes to files. Local VCS’s are a clear improvement over manual version control, and can be useful for small and/or personal projects, but these types of VCS’s still have important limitations:

All file versions are stored locally, making it vulnerable to data loss.
It is difficult to collaborate with others using a local VCS, since changes can’t easily be shared or consolidated.




1.1.2 Centralized VCS
To overcome the limitations with collaborations using local VCS’s, the next type of VCS that was developed was so-called centralized VCS’s. These systems work by have a single central server that contains all the code that is version controlled, and then developers can checkout code from that server and commit changes back to the server. This setup has many advantages over local VCS’s.

Developers knows to some degree what other people are working on.
Administrators can control what files each developer can access and modify.

However, centralized VCS’s still have limitations:

If the server is temporarily offline, nobody can collaborate at all or save new versions of the code.
If the server dies and no proper backup has been kept, the entire history of the project is lost except whatever snapshots of files people happen to have on their local machines.




1.1.3 Distributed VCS
The final type of VCS’s is distributed VCS’s. In a distributed VCS, developers don’t just check out the latest version of files, they make a copy of the entire history of all files. This is a way more flexible type of system that have several advantages over centralized VCS’s.

No single point of failure. Each developer has the entire history of version of files.
Instead of relying on a central server, developers can share and synchronize changes directly with each others.
Developers can work offline.
Allows for more flexible workflows that are not possible with a single centralized server.
Some types of operations are faster since you don’t need to communicate with a server.






Chacon, Scott, and Ben Straub. 2021. “Pro Git.” https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2.

title: 2  Introduction to Git
text:
2.2 How Git stores files
The major difference between Git and most other VCS’s is how Git stores data. In other systems, files are stored as a set of base version of files and the changes made to each file over time. This is commenly called delta-based version control.



Delta-based version control - data is stored as changes to a base version of each file


Git does not think about data this way. Instead, Git thinks of data likes a series of snapshots of a miniature filesystem. With Git, every time you save the state of your project, Git basically takes a picture of how all of your files look like, and stores a reference to that snapshot. to be efficient, if files have not changed, Git doesn’t store that file again, just a link to a previous identical file that it has already stored.



Snapshot version control - data is stored as snapshots at different time points


This is an important distinction between Git and nearly all other VCS’s.

title: 2  Introduction to Git
text:
2.3 File checksumming in Git
Checksumming is a process used to verify the integrity of data. It involves calculating a unique value (the checksum) based on a piece of data, which can then be compared to a checksum calculated from the original data to ensure it hasn’t been altered.
Everything in Git is checksummed before it is stored, and is then referred to by that checksum. That means it is impossible to modify contents of files without Git knowing about it.
The mechanism that Git uses for checksumming is called a SHA-1 hash. This is a 40-characters string composed of hexadecimal characters and calcualted based on the contents of a file or directory sturucture in Git. A SHA-1 hash looks something like this
840552db75d3d52894732b753892427b3f2cafa8

When using Git you will see these hash-values all over the place.

title: 2  Introduction to Git
section: 2.4 The main sections of a Git project
text:
2.4 The main sections of a Git project
TODO: this informatino feels like a re-hash of what is also presented later on in more detail. If this section is kept (in some modified version) add a diagram. Current text is also mostly verbatim taken from pro git p. 15. Needs to be restructured and reworded.
The most important aspect of Git to understand is the three main stages files can be in: modified, staged, and committed.

Modified: Files that have been modified, but has not been committed to the database yet.
Staged: Modified files that have been marked, in their current version, to go into the next commit snapshot.
Committed: Files that are safely stored in your local database are called commited files.

This leads us to the three main sections of a Git project: the working tree, the staging area, and the Git directory.
The working tree is a single checkout of one version of the project. These files are pulled out of the compressed database of the Git directory and placed on disk for you to use or modify.
The staging area is a file, generally contained in your Git directory, that stores information about what willl go into your next commit. Its technical name in Git parlance is the “index”, but the phrase “staging area” works just as well.
The Git directory is where Git stores the metadata and object database for your project. This is the most important part of Git, and it is what is copied when you clone a repository from another computer.
The basic Git workflow goes something like this:

Modify files in working tree.
Selectively stage the changes to be included in the next commit.
Commit the changes

title: 3  Basic Git
text:
3.1 Initializing a Git repository
In this chapter we will cover the basic Git commmands.
So how do we get started using Git? For the moment, we will work from the starting point of having a local directory with files that we want to start version controlling. To do this we cd to the directory and use the git init command. This creates a new subfolder named “.git” that contains all of the necessary repository files - a Git repository skeleton. We will not discuss the contents of the “.git” folder here, but we will get back to that later.
We have now initialized a Git repository, but we are not yet tracking any files. We can start tracking files by using the git add command. Finally, we probably want to commit these files to the Git repository, which can be done with git commit.
At this point we have initiated a Git repository, starting tracking files, and have done an initial commit. We will go over what git add and git commitin detail later, but this is essentially all it takes to use Git in a simple but effective way. Simply keep using git add`` andgit commit` at each point your files are in state that you want to save a version of.

title: 3  Basic Git
text:
3.1.1 Example
In this example we will show how to initialize a Git repository, start tracking files, and commiting them to the repository. First we create a folder with some files that we want to track.
~
$ rm -rf git_example # Remove folder if it already exists
~
$ mkdir git_example # Make folder
~
$ cd git_example # Change working directory
~/git_example
$ echo "file_a content" > file_a.txt # Create file_a.txt
~/git_example
$ echo "file_v content" > file_b.txt # Create file_b.txt

We can now initialize a Git repository, start tracking all the files in the folder, and commit the current state of the files to the repository.
~/git_example
$ git init # Initialize git repo
~/git_example
$ git add . # Track all files
~/git_example
$ git commit -m"Initial commit" # Commit files

title: 3  Basic Git
text:
3.2 Exercises

3.2.1 Exercise - Initiate a Git repository

Intialize a Git repository in a folder.
Start trackin the files in the folder.
Commit the initial state of the files to the repository.

For this exercise you can use any folder of files that you want, for example an existing project that you want to version control using Git. You can also use the below code to create a simple folder with a single file in your home directory.
/c/some/folder
$ cd
~
$ rm -rf git_example
~
$ mkdir git_example
~
$ cd git_example
~/git_example
$ echo "file_a content" > file_a.txt







Solution





The solution assumes the folder we are working in is located at “~/git_example”. If you are using another folder, start by using cd to change the working directory.
~/git_example
$ git init
~/git_example
$ git add .
~/git_example
$ git commit -m"Initial commit"








Chacon, Scott, and Ben Straub. 2021. “Pro Git.” https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2.
